subject: ap-government
theme: Political participation in the United States is shaped by constitutional amendments expanding the franchise, by the linkage institutions — political parties, interest groups, elections, and the media — that connect citizens to government, and by campaign finance rules that govern how money flows through the electoral system.

core_concepts.title: Expansion of Voting Rights
core_concepts.detail: Two key amendments expanded the franchise in the 20th century: the Twenty-Fourth Amendment (1964) abolished poll taxes in federal elections, removing a financial barrier used to suppress voting in the South, and the Twenty-Sixth Amendment (1971) lowered the national voting age from 21 to 18 — partly in response to Vietnam-era arguments that those old enough to be drafted were old enough to vote.

core_concepts.title: Voter Turnout & Decision Models
core_concepts.detail: Voter Turnout — the share of eligible voters casting ballots — is shaped by Political Efficacy (the belief that one's participation matters and government will respond). Once at the ballot, voters use different decision models: Rational Choice Voting (cost-benefit analysis of which candidate serves their interests), Retrospective Voting (judging the incumbent's past performance), Prospective Voting (judging future promises), Party-Line Voting (one party top to bottom), or Split Ticket Voting (mixing parties across offices).

core_concepts.title: Parties as Linkage Institutions
core_concepts.detail: A Political Party is one of the four main Linkage Institutions (along with interest groups, elections, and media) connecting citizen preferences to government policy. Parties write a Party Platform at the national convention and select nominees through a Primary Election — either an Open Primary, in which any registered voter may choose a party's ballot, or a Closed Primary, in which only voters registered with that party may vote.

core_concepts.title: Electoral System Structure
core_concepts.detail: U.S. House and most state elections use Single Member Districts paired with a Winner-Take-All System: only the top vote-getter wins, leaving runners-up unrepresented and reinforcing two major parties. Many other democracies use Proportional Representation, allocating seats to parties in proportion to vote share. In presidential races, the Winner-Take-All System concentrates campaign resources on a small number of Swing States where the two major parties are closely matched.

core_concepts.title: Interest Group Strategies
core_concepts.detail: Interest groups take many forms — Ideological Groups (organized around a political philosophy), Public Interest Groups (advocating for the broad public), Single Issue Groups (focused on a single cause), and Professional Associations (representing a profession such as the AMA or ABA). They influence policy through Lobbying (direct contact with officials) and Grassroots Lobbying (mobilizing ordinary citizens to pressure their representatives). Stable Iron Triangle relationships (a congressional committee, an executive agency, and an interest group) and looser Issue Networks structure how groups, government, and experts shape policy in specific areas.

core_concepts.title: Campaign Finance & Citizens United
core_concepts.detail: A Political Action Committee collects and distributes regulated money from a corporation, union, or interest group to federal candidates. In Citizens United v. FEC (2010), the Supreme Court ruled that corporations and unions have First Amendment rights to spend unlimited funds on independent political expenditures — creating Super PACs, which can raise unlimited money from any source but cannot coordinate directly with candidates or parties.

core_concepts.title: Media as a Linkage Institution
core_concepts.detail: The First Amendment's protection of a Free Press allows news organizations to publish without government censorship, making the media a key linkage institution between citizens and government. The media acts as a Gatekeeper by selecting which stories, issues, and candidates reach the public, and election coverage often takes the form of Horse Race Journalism — focusing on polls, strategy, and who is winning or losing rather than substantive policy.

key_terms.definition: An organized group that seeks to win elections and control government by nominating candidates and coordinating their campaigns around a shared ideology or platform

Independent expenditure-only committees that may raise unlimited funds from individuals, corporations, and unions to spend on political advertising, but cannot coordinate directly with candidates or parties
The process by which a state's registered voters in a party vote to select delegates to the national convention or choose among candidates for the party's presidential nomination
The process by which interest groups attempt to influence legislators and government officials by making direct contact, providing information, and building relationships
The 2010 Supreme Court case that ruled corporations and unions have First Amendment rights to spend unlimited funds on independent political expenditures

exam_tip: The AP exam heavily tests the connection between Citizens United v. FEC and the First Amendment — know that the Court extended free speech rights to corporations and unions for independent expenditures (not direct contributions), and always connect campaign finance questions to the distinction between Super PACs (unlimited independent spending) and a Political Action Committee (regulated direct contributions to candidates).
